Literature Title: Exploring Equity in Healthcare Services: Spatial Accessibility Changes during Subway Expansion
Accessibility Method: gravity-based accessibility, cumulative opportunity accessibility, utility-based accessibility, emerging measures based on real-time individual data
Demand Population: population-based demand data from Shenyang Statistical Yearbook
Transport Mode: PT (public transportation), Shenyang Rail Transit, bus lines
Travel Time Period: different stages of subway system development

Literature Title: Measuring geographic access to health care: raster and network-based methods
Accessibility Method: raster and network (vector) based models of spatial data representation
Facility Type: Minor collectors, Rural arterials and major collectors, Rural local, Urban, state owned arterials and major collectors, Urban, county owned arterials and major collectors, Urban, state owned local, Urban, county primary local, Urban, county local
Demand Population: people residing more than 30 minutes from an acute care hospital
Dist/Time Calc Method: Travel time using cell dimensions and travel speed in raster model, Travel time estimates in network data model

Literature Title: A relative spatial access assessment approach for analyzing potential spatial access to colorectal cancer services in Texas
Study Area & Country: corridor area containing nine connecting counties in Texas, USA
Accessibility Method: gravity model, enhanced 2-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA)
Facility Type: CRC services, primary care physicians, CRC screening facilities, oncologists
Demand Population: Population size, racial/ethnic distribution, poverty rate, rural/urban status
Dist/Time Calc Method: travel cost from i to j, travel time between j and k, travel time between Dr and the service site
Urbanization Rate: Increased rurality corresponded to decreased potential spatial access

Literature Title: Spatial Accessibility Evaluation and Location Optimization of Primary Healthcare in China: A Case Study of Shenzhen
Facility Type: Primary health institutions (PHIs), Secondary hospitals, Tertiary hospitals
Demand Population: Population that CHCs need to cater to, calculated by combining residential points with census data

Literature Title: Challenging terrains: socio-spatial analysis of Primary Health Care Access Disparities in West Virginia
Accessibility Method: modified enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2FCA)
Facility Type: Hospitals, Medical institutions, OBGYN/Birthing centers, Primary care clinics, Pediatrics Physicians, Behavioral providers, Rehabilitation services, Specialty care providers
Demand Population: American Community Survey 2016, a five-year estimate between 2012 and 2015
Dist/Time Calc Method: improving the accuracy of the analysis regarding measuring distance, considering distance decay effect, and more precisely representing the location of supply and demand
Transport Mode: Public transportation or a private vehicle
Travel Time Period: 10 minutes, up to 25 mph, between 25 and 45 mph, faster than 45 mph

Literature Title: Understanding inequalities in spatial accessibility to multi-tier healthcare for older adults in rapidly aging Bangladesh
Accessibility Method: travel distance or time, population ratios, gravity-based approaches, including a family of two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) methods
Facility Type: Community Clinics (CC), Upazila Health — Complexes (UHC), District Hospitals (DH), Emergency health services
Dist/Time Calc Method: travel distance or time, population ratios, gravity-based approaches, including a family of two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) methods
Transport Mode: walking, rickshaws, CNG auto-rickshaws, human haulers, buses
Travel Time Period: primary healthcare: 1.56 h, secondary and tertiary healthcare: 2.69 and 5.06 h

Literature Title: Iowa spatial accessibility to screening mammography is improving, but utilization among privately insured women remains stable, 2016‑2022
Study Area & Country: Urban Core, Suburban, Large Rural Town, Small Town, and Rural. Iowa, USA
Demand Population: women of recommended screening age, population that could not be allocated by the model
Dist/Time Calc Method: driving distance and time along the road network
Urbanization Rate: Urban population is increasing over time, while the rural, small town, and large rural town population are decreasing

Accessibility Method: 2-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA), 2-Step Virtual Catchment Area (2SVCA)

Literature Title: Integrating Spatial and Non-Spatial Dimensions to Evaluate Access to Rural Primary Healthcare Service: A Case Study of Songzi, China
Accessibility Method: Gaussian two-step floating catchment area (G-2SFCA) method, questionnaire survey, comprehensive evaluation index system
Facility Type: clinics (Grade I hospitals), general hospitals (Grade II or III hospitals)
Demand Population: patients, including demographic, socio-economic characters, and patients’ level of satisfaction with medical services
Travel Time Period: 15 min for Grade I hospitals, 30 min for Grade II and III hospitals

Literature Title: Evaluating Disparities in Elderly Community Care Resources Using a Geographic Accessibility and Inequality Index
Study Area & Country: Pingtung County, Taiwan; Taitung County, Taiwan; Hualien County, Taiwan; Keelung City, Taiwan; Hsinchu City, Taiwan; Chiayi City, Taiwan; Taipei City, Taiwan
Accessibility Method: M1 Nearest road distance, M2 Nearest distance supplier loading (people), M3 Accessibility of ownership averaged by administrative districts, M4 Accessibility of nearest simple distance-decay
Facility Type: community care center, community-based care resources, elderly community center
Demand Population: Pi (demand population over 65 years in village i)
Dist/Time Calc Method: nearest distance-decay effect, considering within-jurisdiction accessible resources and the nearest road distances, considering the supplier’s loading, accounting for village population and supplier loading, setting 3 km as the appropriate nearest supplier’s distance

Literature Title: Determining spatial access to opioid use disorder treatment and emergency medical services in New Hampshire
Study Area & Country: Baltimore, USA; New York City, USA; Houston, USA
Accessibility Method: enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) model and the Huff model
Facility Type: treatment facilities, buprenorphine treatment practitioners, fire stations with Safe Station programs, EMS stations with multiple service vehicles
Demand Population: individuals who died from unintentional overdoses involving fentanyl
Dist/Time Calc Method: street network distances, driving times and distance decay relationships
Transport Mode: not explicitly stated but implied by the use of driving time
Travel Time Period: not explicitly stated but implied by the use of driving time
Urbanization Rate: metropolitan, micropolitan, small town, and rural areas

Literature Title: Comparing geographical distribution of community‑based physiotherapists and family physicians across Saskatchewan
Demand Population: individuals below the after-tax low-income measure (LIM-AT); population aged 65þ; population aged 15þ without certificate, diploma, or degree; immigrants who came to Canada from 2001 to 2011; aboriginal ethnicity
